title: Interview With 'Hidden Figures' Director Ted Melfi | The Big Picture (Ep. 1)
format: Sean Fennessey talks to Ted Melfi about Hidden Figures, the year it took to make, and how a movie about math and three black women became the number one film in the country.

films.title: Hidden Figures
films.note: The number one movie in the country the weekend they talked. Adapted from Margot Lee Shetterly's book about Katherine Johnson, Dorothy Vaughan, and Mary Jackson, the NASA mathematicians behind the Mercury missions. Melfi wrote it off a 55-page book proposal and a first draft by Allison Schroeder, finished the script in December 2015, and was shooting by March 2016; Shetterly's finished book arrived mid-shoot and he refused to read it. He spent a month writing the scene where Jim Parsons explains re-entry math, and the film's last third is verbatim NASA transcripts. Pharrell wrote the score and songs; Hans Zimmer and Ben Wallfisch composed.

films.title: St. Vincent
films.note: Melfi's prior feature, with Bill Murray. He'd wanted Taraji P. Henson for it, and contrasts Murray's live-in-the-moment freedom with Kevin Costner's meticulous planning.

films.title: The Curious Case of Benjamin Button
films.note: Where Melfi first fell for Taraji P. Henson; she got an Oscar nomination for it.

films.title: Loving
films.note: Named alongside Fences and Moonlight as proof the wave of 2016 films wasn't a reaction to Oscars So White; Hollywood had been developing them for years.

films.title: The Birth of a Nation
films.note: Melfi notes Nate Parker had been working on it for eight years.

films.title: Moonlight
films.note: Cited among the long-gestating films that only looked like a coordinated response.

films.title: Fences
films.note: Melfi points to how long Denzel had been circling it. Later the subject of the 'Hidden Fences' red-carpet flubs.

films.title: Going in Style
films.note: Where Melfi befriended Dustin Hoffman, who called about Hidden Figures, though Hoffman didn't end up in the film.

films.title: The Jungle Book
films.note: His example of a big-budget studio movie that still carries a moral; he praises Jon Favreau's version.

Not films: Hidden Fences (the red-carpet title flub by Jenna Bush Hager and Michael Keaton, not a real movie)

The Curious Case of Benjamin Button.title: The Curious Case of Benjamin Button

Precious.title: Precious: Based on the Novel 'Push' by Sapphire
